story.title: Dr. Chen and the Case of the Connected Metals
story.text:
My name is Dr. Sarah Chen. I am a scientist, and I study complex systems. I look at how different parts work together, like pieces of a puzzle. Right now, I am studying Rare Earth Metals. These special metals are in your phone, computer, and electric cars.

Will we run out of them? To find the answer, I look at how the system works. Let's break it down.

1. First, we look at the supply. The Mining Rate adds new metals from the ground. The Recycling Rate also adds metals back from old electronics. Both of these things increase the available supply for everyone.

2. Next, we look at the price. When the supply of a metal is high, its price is low. But when the supply is low, the metal becomes rare and its price goes up.

3. Then, we see how companies react to high prices. A high price creates a Substitution Incentive. This is a reason for companies to find new, cheaper materials to use instead. This helps save the rare metals we have left.

My job is to understand how all these pieces connect. This helps us make smart choices for our technology and our planet's future.
think_abouts: What happens to the price of a metal when there is a lot of it?
Why would a company want to use a different material if rare earth metals get too expensive?

margin_defs.definition: A set of connected parts that work together.
The amount of something that is available to use.

check_prompts: What are two ways to increase the supply of rare earth metals?

vocabulary.definition: A special group of 17 metals needed for technology like phones and computers.
vocabulary.use_sentence: My new phone works because it has tiny amounts of Rare Earth Metals inside.

vocabulary.definition: How fast we dig materials like metals out of the ground.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The high Mining Rate for these metals could cause problems later.

vocabulary.definition: The percentage of old products we collect to reuse their materials.
vocabulary.use_sentence: Our city has a good Recycling Rate for plastic bottles.

vocabulary.definition: A reason, usually related to cost, to find a replacement for a material.
vocabulary.use_sentence: When the price went up, there was a Substitution Incentive to use a different material.

discussion_cards.prompt: If you were in charge of a big electronics company, would you spend more money on recycling old phones or on finding new places to mine? Explain your choice.
discussion_cards.frame: If I were in charge, I would choose to spend more money on ___ because ___.

discussion_cards.prompt: Dr. Chen says high prices create a 'substitution incentive.' What is one new material you think scientists could invent to replace rare earth metals in phones?
discussion_cards.frame: I think scientists could invent a material that is ___ to replace rare earth metals.

reflection_frames.knew: Before reading, I already knew that ___
reflection_frames.learned: From the story, I learned that ___
